sentences.text: Go ahead-- on-- first door on your left.
No, no, no, after that.
You go--
--all around, as far as you can go.
Just don't look at the mess.
[LAUGHS]
I was born on July 19, 1926, in Czechoslovakia, a small town called Munkacs.
In Hungarian, it's called Munkacs.
In Czechoslovakian, it's called Mukacevo.
And the pronunciation is, is what makes the difference.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] that's, that's about-- that's where I grew up.
Town was about 32,000 people-- the total.
It was about half and half-- 16,000 Jews, 16,000 non-Jews.
Yes, my father was religious.
Orthodox, yes.
Yes, my grandfather had a kaftan, you know, that black jacket with a-- with a, a shtreimel, you know, and white socks like the rabbis get dressed.
But my father was just modern, but Orthodox.
You know, he-- you couldn't turn on the light on Friday night or Saturday.
Yes.
My father's name was Bernard.
My mother's name was Regina.
I had an-- my oldest sister was Margaret.
She is still alive.
She is-- lives in Montreal now.
And then I had an older brother, David.
After that, it was me.
And after myself-- and there was my younger brother, Ernest, and still another younger brother.
He was Tibor.
We called him [NON-ENGLISH].
Yes, they all had nicknames.
[LAUGHS] My brother David had the nickname [NON-ENGLISH].
My, my nickname was [NON-ENGLISH].
My younger brother, Ernest, was [NON-ENGLISH].
And the youngest brother was [NON-ENGLISH].
Yes.
Yes.
Mostly Hungarian, really.
My, my-- when my parents were born in that region, it used to belong to Hungary.
And so we spoke mostly Hungarian and Yiddish.
That makes me an old man.
[LAUGHTER]
When the war broke out in Poland, you mean, in 1938?
I was going to school.
I went to school.
I don't--
Well, we were glued to the radio all the time-- what the Germans are doing today, and tomorrow, and the next day, and so on.
And the propaganda that they were issuing-- it worked in many different ways.
We were listening to the radio when they made a pact with Russia.
And then there weren't that many radios in households.
We had a lot of neighbors come over and listen to the radio at our house.
There was no television, of course.
Well, it was-- it was frightening that the Germans-- they were spewing hatred, especially against the Jews.
And so that's why we listened so intently-- intensely what's going on with the Germans.
No.
No.
There was-- I grew up with it.
I grew up with it.
Because in that small town that we lived in, there were, as I said, 16,000 non-Jews.
Many of them were Russian.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And many of them were Schwabs.
Schwab is like German.
And I was afraid many times just to walk to school because, you know, you-- they beat you up.
They were stronger.
There were more of them, and so on.
So it wasn't the most pleasant childhood that I had as far as growing up and going to school, and so on.
But--
Oh, yes.
Yes.
Just like I'm finding out here, the colored people are aware of the fact that they are a different skin, which, to me, was a surprise when I came to this country-- that they are treated differently.
But that's-- has nothing to do with the interview.
[LAUGHS]
No, we dealt with it.
We dealt with it.
I fought back--
--if I was called names and this and that.
I, I was-- I was caught many times in fights in school.
My-- we had-- actually, we had a good life.
My father had a-- had a, like, a liquor store.
And he provided for all of us.
We had a comfortable life.
Every summer, we went away for, for the entire summer.
It was like what you would call here 'vacation.' It, it was in a winery, where it was a lot cooler.
And it was just vacationing.
There was no school, you know.
And I, I always thought it was-- it was a good life.
While I said that it was-- it was hard as far as growing up with anti-Semitism, but it was-- it wasn't a difficult life.
We, we lived with it.
Yes, yeah, yes, until later on, you know, when the-- when the war broke out and Czechoslovakia has become Hungary.
In 1938, Hitler ordered this part of Czechoslovakia to be returned to Hungary.
So instead of Czech school, I started going to Hungarian school.
And to me, the transition wasn't that difficult because I already spoke Hungarian.
And we knew how to read and write Hungarian, and so on.
We heard about Hitler from the very first-- from 1933, when he was making his, his rise.
[DRINKS]
Yes, they were.
And, as time went on, later on, people came from that region.
Either from Poland, they made their escape and told us horrible stories.
And we just didn't want to believe it.
Yeah.
Yes.
I think so.
There are a few times I saw her in the store.
[LAUGHS]
Well, this was with other-- with other brothers of my father and their families.
And so it wasn't just my family alone that went to this winery, and so on.
And it was just a lot of fun because there were a lot of us.
And as time went on-- I think I was, like, 16 or 17 when my aunt from Romania asked my father to send me one of your boys to help me out.
Because she had three sons older.
And all three sons were drafted into the Hungarian Army.
And here she had a business of a stationary store.
And she also had a business of a printing shop.
Like, she printed the only placard-- what-- what's playing in the movies next week, and so on, and business cards, books, and whatever a printer is printing.
And so she couldn't handle it all.
So I volunteered that I will go there.
It seemed like an adventure to go to a different town.
It's--
I don't really know mileage.
That's why I'm going to call my cousin.
He-- it, it was a good day's travel by train.
So it wasn't like, like, you can come back anytime, and so on.
And--
Sighet.
Elie Wiesel is from, yes.
Yes, yes.
They were well known.
Their name was Kaufman.
And they were a, a prominent family in that town.
It was also a small town, smaller than Munkacs.
No.
Also, I would say probably half and half.
No.
[LAUGHS]
[LAUGHS] When you say 'get along,' I don't exactly know what you--
Well, yes, they coexisted, of course.
No, they weren't isolated.
They coexisted.
And you, you put up a front.
But then, then the dislike was down underneath.
In many-- in many ways, you could detect that-- not all the time, of course.
But--
No.
No.
If, if there were, I'm not aware of it.
I, I just don't know.
Maybe I was too young to know that.
My aunt's name was Kaufman.
Her, her name was Janka, J-A-N-K-A.
I was 17, yeah.
It was  in '43.
I was either 16 or 17.
I've got to figure it out.
[LAUGHS]
I was there for-- I was taken from there by the Germans.
I wanted to go back home-- to make my way back home as things got worse, and worse, and worse.
And by that time, I just couldn't.
I learned how to-- how to print [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a, a letter of furlough, for example, for the army.
I was making the official stamps for the Hungarian Army there, like the rubber stamp.
And, and we printed the furlough papers.
That's how her three boys were able to come home many times on a furlough, even though they were not on furlough.
They just paid off the sergeant.
And he covered for them for a week or so.
Yes, yes.
And I printed a paper for myself, like a birth certificate.
I did that at 4 o'clock in the morning.
No workers were there.
I picked out the letters myself.
I put-- locked it in the machine.
And I printed it.
And I made myself a non-Jewish-sounding name.
And so therefore, I figured I'm able to travel on the train.
Because they were taking off Jews from the train.
No, I-- nobody knew that.
And I didn't do it for anyone except for myself.
And my aunt found out.
She started to cry.
She wouldn't let me go.
She said, you're the only man in the family.

And you heard what the Germans are-- this is already later on, when the Germans were promising you this-- that we're all taking you to one town somewhere in Hungary where all the Jews will be assembled in that one town.
And so you'll meet your parents there at that time.
I just wanted to make my way back home to Munkacs.
It was about a year-- about a year.
I hadn't been home at all.
No, they entered-- I think it was in March.
They-- but the directives that were issued maybe two weeks later-- all the Jews have to wear an arm band.
Another week later, all the Jews have to wear a, a-- the Star, Star of David.
Yes.
Yes, yes.
And all the Jews have to move to another part of town, which was the ghetto.
Nobody was allowed in or out of there.
And it just so happens I was allowed to go out because, as I said, I was printing for the Hungarian Army all kinds of papers that they needed.
It was just several streets that were secluded, yeah.
Yes.
I don't really know.
I know that we had to go out in the middle of the street one day.
It was in early May.
And a few hours later, they loaded us onto boxcars.
And three days later, I wound up in a prison camp.
No.
They just came around.
And, and it wasn't even the, the, the Hungarian police.
The gendarmes, they were the worst.
They were worse than, than the Germans themselves.
They brought in police from different cities so that you don't know privately these people that you are dealing with right now, and so on.
They were just terrible.
[SIGHS] Because they were--
They were hitting-- beating you with the gun butts.
Just, just making it, you know-- they didn't have to do these things on their own.
Nobody was behind them, watching them, and so on.
They just did it because they were sadistic.
They were happy to see the Jews get out of there.
I'm just adding that as a guess.
It was not possible--
--just--
No.
In the beginning, we were writing to each other, you know.
My mother was very proud of me because my aunt wrote all kinds of nice things about me.
My aunt was very religious.
You couldn't even go to a movie.
And I was working-- I loved what I learned in the printing shop.
And I was working there on my own.
I did things on my own.
Yes.
Well, she had her daughter-in-law there.
She had a little boy, three years old-- the daughter-in-law-- who loved me to pieces.
That's one of the reasons why she was able to convince me not to leave.
And they're going to take you off the train.
I said, no, they're not.
I'm not a Jew.
And I pulled out my paper.
And I showed her what I printed for myself.
And then she even got scared worse.
They're going to catch you.
They're going to shoot you right there.
And so I told her, chances have to be taken.
I want to get home.
And--
[DRINKS]
Yes, I was afraid.
But still, you do things even though you are afraid.
If you-- if the end-- the goal is you want it that bad-- and I wanted to go home.
All I remember was a lot of shouting and yelling and screaming, and whatnot.
And the three boys that my aunt had that were drafted in the army-- the youngest one heard what's going on, and so on.
And he, he made his way home through one of those furlough papers that I printed.
He wanted to say goodbye to his girlfriend.
He was about three years older than I was, maybe, maybe four.
And the Germans caught him.
And they threw him in the-- in the boxcars.
They beat him up and threw him in the boxcars.
He wound up in, in Auschwitz, also.
In fact, he had the number-- my number is 4344.
His number was 4345.
And his brother-in-law's number-- he had older sisters that were married-- his brother-in-law's number was 4343.
He was with me in the camp all throughout-- almost to the end.
The reason I say almost-- we got separated in Buchenwald.
That was to-- almost near the end.
Yes, he lives in Haifa, Israel.
I went to visit him.
He was very happy to see me.
And, and this younger-- the younger son that came to say goodbye to his family, he never made it.
After three weeks, we got separated in Auschwitz.
I don't know how many people there were, but we were packed in like herrings practically.
Just-- it was just suffocating, hot, smelly.
They-- you had your bathroom right there.
And you didn't go out.
They didn't ever open the doors.
And if somebody was screaming that somebody had died or something, they still didn't open the doors.
So it, it was just a horrible trip that I, I want to forget.
I can't.
I don't want to forget.
But I want to forget.
Yeah, I think they gave you water.
We had-- we had some food that we were allowed to bring with us.
But they didn't give us any food.
Three days-- three days and two nights, two nights and three days, something like that.
Or maybe three nights and two days.
I don't remember.
It took a total of three days.
We arrived in Birkenau.
We arrived into a camp, where we're [SIGHS] surrounded by barbed wire.
And as you got off the train-- and everybody was, schnell, schnell!
Los, los!
Everything had to be done fast.
I don't care how fast you did something, it wasn't fast enough for the Germans.
And and there was a-- one person that looked like a ranked German, with high rank.
And he was telling you, you go to this side.
You go to this side, and so on.
And later on, we found out the left side meant-- or one side meant life.
The other side meant the death camp.
Oh, as soon as we, we got inside.
I was inside the camp already with a striped Sing-Sing type of suit as a prisoner.
One of the barrack leaders, who was a Polish Jew, also a prisoner-- but he was already there since 1938 or so.
He said, that's your parents burning there.
And I just-- I said, you're crazy!
He said, yeah.
I spit in his face.
I literally spit in his face.
He started beating me up.
Yes.
Yes, he was.
And I even yelled at him.
I said, why are you saying that?
You're a Jew.
I mean, why, why are you saying these things?
And the reason they left me there-- they would've beat me to death.
All of a sudden, it, it just started pouring rain out of the clear blue.
It just was raining.
And they started to get wet.
Yes, yes.
In Auschwitz, the very first day, they tattooed me, which wasn't a picnic in itself either.
I didn't have permanent injuries.
Fortunately, the, the, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the leader of the barrack-- he picked up a shovel.
And he was beating me with the handle of the shovel.
And at one point, he hit me right here on the bone.
And the shovel broke-- the handle.
So he just left me.
And just at that time is when the Germans arrived.
One of the reasons why I wound up there in that barrack-- as I said, I was taken from Sighet.
I wound up among total strangers.
They spoke in Romanian among themselves.
I didn't even understand what they're talking about.
Now, they were Jews, mind you.
But they spoke Romanian.
And here and there, I heard some Jewish words.
And I said to myself, I'm just going to die here.
I have to find some people from my hometown.
Because word gets around that people arrived yesterday from Munkacs-- or today, and so on.
And I go from barrack to barrack to barrack looking for some of the people that I am familiar with.
And sure enough, I found-- I found a barrack with-- full of kids from Munkacs, classmates of mine.
A couple hours later--
I think I was 17, yeah.
A couple of hours later, some high-ranking [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people came in.
And they make an announcement that one person-- there's, there's one person too many in this barrack.
And from barrack number so-and-so, there's one person missing.
And he started-- and that's when the Germans started hitting me.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as I said, as, as a punishment, I went to Auschwitz, where they tattooed me.
I was there for three weeks just doing nothing.
And--
All right.
I saw him tap you on the shoulder.
[LAUGHS]
